decision_scenario: Lo! Foods gives you three choices for Protein Chef going forward: (a) Fresh, no preservatives, 3-day shelf life, ₹99; (b) Clean-preserved with rosemary extract, 7-day shelf life, ₹99; (c) Frozen at source, no preservatives, 30-day freezer life, ₹109. Which do you choose for your regular weekly purchase — and which format, if any, would make you buy Protein Chef more frequently than you currently do?

rounds.stimulus: You're ordering Protein Chef on Blinkit. The listing now clearly states: 'No preservatives — freshly baked, 3-day shelf life. For best taste, consume within 72 hours of delivery.' You buy bread roughly once a week. Your Blinkit delivery usually arrives within the hour. Does the 3-day shelf life feel like a selling point (fresh, clean), a limitation (you might waste it), or something that doesn't affect your decision?

Lo! Foods sends an email: 'We've listened to your feedback. Introducing the new Protein Chef — same recipe, same protein, same no-maida promise, but now with rosemary extract and vitamin E (both from natural sources) to extend shelf life to 7 days. No artificial preservatives. Price unchanged at ₹99.' The packaging will now say 'Clean-preserved for freshness.' How do you feel about this change — does 'natural preservatives' change your perception of the product's cleanliness, or does it solve a real problem for you?

Lo! Foods launches 'Protein Chef Frozen' — identical recipe to the fresh version, no preservatives at all, flash-frozen at the bakery within 2 hours of baking. Shelf life: 30 days in freezer. Thaw overnight in fridge, or 2 minutes in microwave. Available on Blinkit at ₹109 (delivered in a cold pack) and as a monthly subscription at ₹89/loaf. The campaign says: 'The freshest bread you've ever had — locked at peak freshness.' Does frozen solve the shelf life problem without compromising your sense of 'clean food'?
rounds.decision: I choose option (b) — clean-preserved with rosemary extract, 7-day shelf life, ₹99 — for my regular weekly purchase. It fits my actual shopping pattern, it's the same price as the fresh option, and Lo! Foods has been transparent about what they're using (rosemary extract — not some vague 'natural flavor' claim). I'm buying from a brand I already trust, and this format doesn't require me to change how I cook, how I store food, or how I plan my week. As for what would make me buy Protein Chef MORE frequently: honestly, the frozen format at ₹109 has that potential — but only if I see real peer reviews confirming the texture and taste hold up after thawing. If my neighbors or an online community I trust validated the frozen experience, I'd switch to that and potentially stock up — buying twice a month instead of weekly, which would actually reduce my per-trip effort. The subscription angle at ₹89 I recall being mentioned is compelling if it applies to frozen, because that's where the math starts working in my favor. But I need evidence before I commit to a format change.
